---
title: "why is my xubuntu14.04LTS desktop able to resolve hostnames?"
date: 2016-02-12
forum: Networking &amp; Wireless
---

### Post by r.stiltskin on 2016-02-12
I guess that sounds like a silly question.  :)

OK, so ... /etc/network/interfaces contains just these 3 lines:
```
# interfaces(5) file used by ifup(8) and ifdown(8)
auto lo
iface lo inet loopback

```
and /etc/resolv.conf is just
```
# Dynamic resolv.conf(5) file for glibc resolver(3) generated by resolvconf(8)
#     DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE BY HAND -- YOUR CHANGES WILL BE OVERWRITTEN
nameserver 127.0.1.1

```In older versions of Ubuntu and other distros that I've installed in previous years these files had more information, and in particular /etc/resolv.conf contained addresses of nameservers obtained from the router and sometimes nameservers that I wrote in manually.  Yet without any of that, this desktop machine has no trouble resolving everything on the internet.  So by what magic is this working?

man resolvconf is not really giving me a satisfactory explanation, so is there anything else I can read that will explain this?

Aside from general curiosity, I have another good reason for this question.  I'm trying to figure out why my beaglebone black, running an arm port of ubuntu 14.04, connected to the same router, is unable to resolve any hostnames unless I manually edit resolv.conf and provide a nameserver.  If for example I add "nameserver [ip address of router]" or "nameserver 8.8.8.8" to the file, everything is fine, but  the auto-generated resolv.conf is no good.  The BBB's resolv.conf contains the following:
```
# Dynamic resolv.conf(5) file for glibc resolver(3) generated by resolvconf(8)
#     DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE BY HAND -- YOUR CHANGES WILL BE OVERWRITTEN
nameserver 127.0.0.1
domain localdomain
search localdomain

```

Any ideas?

---

### Post by bab1 on 2016-02-12
> **r.stiltskin said:**
> I guess that sounds like a silly question.  :)

OK, so ... /etc/network/interfaces contains just these 3 lines:
```
# interfaces(5) file used by ifup(8) and ifdown(8)
auto lo
iface lo inet loopback

```
and /etc/resolv.conf is just
```
# Dynamic resolv.conf(5) file for glibc resolver(3) generated by resolvconf(8)
#     DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE BY HAND -- YOUR CHANGES WILL BE OVERWRITTEN
nameserver 127.0.1.1

```In older versions of Ubuntu and other distros that I've installed in previous years these files had more information, and in particular /etc/resolv.conf contained addresses of nameservers obtained from the router and sometimes nameservers that I wrote in manually.  Yet without any of that, this desktop machine has no trouble resolving everything on the internet.  So by what magic is this working?

man resolvconf is not really giving me a satisfactory explanation, so is there anything else I can read that will explain this?

Recent versions of Ubuntu now use Network-Manager to configure the IP networking.  In addition there is a minimal version of DNS-Masq thta is a caching DNS server installed.  Since it is local to the Ubuntu host it can run via the loopback interface (127.0.1.1).  All unknown DNS request are forwarded upstream for DNS resolution and the results cached. 
> 
Aside from general curiosity, I have another good reason for this question.  I'm trying to figure out why my beaglebone black, running an arm port of ubuntu 14.04, connected to the same router, is unable to resolve any hostnames unless I manually edit resolv.conf and provide a nameserver.  If for example I add "nameserver [ip address of router]" or "nameserver 8.8.8.8" to the file, everything is fine, but  the auto-generated resolv.conf is no good.  The BBB's resolv.conf contains the following:
```
# Dynamic resolv.conf(5) file for glibc resolver(3) generated by resolvconf(8)
#     DO NOT EDIT THIS FILE BY HAND -- YOUR CHANGES WILL BE OVERWRITTEN
nameserver 127.0.0.1
domain localdomain
search localdomain

```

Any ideas?
No caching DNS server on on the BBB install.  You can add the nameservers in the /etc/resolvconf/resolv.conf.d/head file so you don't have to add them each time you boot the BBB.

---

### Post by r.stiltskin on 2016-02-12
Thanks, that clears it up some, but I still don't understand fully.

Is it true that on older systems, a dhclient script obtained nameserver addresses from the router and supplied them to resolvconf, and resolvconv in turn added those nameservers to the resolv.conf file?

Is resolvconf not used at all on these recent Ubuntu systems?

I see that dnsmasq is running on the BBB (ps -e | grep dnsmasq shows that).  If it's not caching DNS info, what is it doing?

Can you recommend anything that I can read that explains (at least in general term) how these programs are being used, in recent systems and, say 10 years ago, short of trying to slog my way through every script that runs during boot-up?

---

### Post by bab1 on 2016-02-12
> **r.stiltskin said:**
> Thanks, that clears it up some, but I still don't understand fully.

Is it true that on older systems, a dhclient script obtained nameserver addresses from the router and supplied them to resolvconf, and resolvconv in turn added those nameservers to the resolv.conf file?

Is resolvconf not used at all on these recent Ubuntu systems?

I have not modified or inspected the dhclient scripts myself.  I only use dhcp with my wireless laptops.  These are configured via Network Manager.  I can tell you that whatever I do, the local striped down version of DNSmasq always runs as a chaching DNS server on the local host.  The resolveconf routines (at /etc/resolvconf) are still used.  What is not done anymore is the manual configuration of the /etc/resolv.conf file (note the dot (.) in the file name.
> 
I see that dnsmasq is running on the BBB (ps -e | grep dnsmasq shows that).  If it's not caching DNS info, what is it doing?
the DNSmasq that is "baked in" should be caching DNS info.  It is not configurable at all.
> 
Can you recommend anything that I can read that explains (at least in general term) how these programs are being used, in recent systems and, say 10 years ago, short of trying to slog my way through every script that runs during boot-up?
How it all works is basically described [**here**]("https://www.stgraber.org/2012/02/24/dns-in-ubuntu-12-04/").

---
